sentiment.rationale: God comes down in judgment upon Samaria and Judah for their transgressions, with a lament over the cities being destroyed.
Woe upon those who devise iniquity and steal fields; false prophets are condemned, yet a brief promise of future gathering.
Harsh rebuke of corrupt leaders, false prophets, and judges who build Zion with blood; Jerusalem will become heaps.
A glorious vision of the mountain of the Lord's house established, nations flowing to it, and swords beaten into plowshares.
Prophesies the Messiah's birth in Bethlehem and His rule from everlasting; the remnant of Jacob shall be among the nations.
God's controversy with His people recounting His mercies; the requirements of justice, mercy, and humility; judgment for dishonesty.
Begins with lamentation over wickedness but ends with confidence in God's faithfulness, pardoning iniquity, and casting sins into the sea.

summaries.summary: God descends in judgment upon Samaria and Judah for idolatry; Micah laments with wordplay over the cities that will be devastated.
Woe to those who plot evil and seize others' property; false prophets speak comforting lies, yet God promises to gather a remnant like sheep.
Micah condemns the rulers who devour the people, the prophets who mislead for profit, and declares that Jerusalem shall become heaps.
In the last days the mountain of the Lord's house shall be established, nations will learn war no more, and the lame will become a strong nation.
Out of Bethlehem shall come a ruler whose goings forth have been from everlasting; the remnant of Jacob shall be as dew from the Lord.
God sets forth His case against Israel, reminding them of His mercies; He requires justice, mercy, and humility rather than rivers of oil.
Micah mourns the scarcity of righteous men but closes with trust in God's forgiveness, compassion, and faithfulness to Abraham.

crossReferences.note: Nearly identical prophecy of the mountain of the Lord's house being established in the top of the mountains in the last days.
Swords into plowshares and spears into pruninghooks — shared word-for-word with Isaiah.
Quoted by the chief priests when Herod inquired where the Christ should be born.
Jesus rebukes the Pharisees for neglecting judgment, mercy, and faith — echoing Micah's triad.

verses.reason: Prophesies Jerusalem shall become heaps — quoted by the elders in Jeremiah 26:18 to save Jeremiah's life.
'Come, and let us go up to the mountain of the Lord' — the temple gathering prophecy shared with Isaiah.
'But thou, Beth-lehem Ephratah... out of thee shall he come forth unto me that is to be ruler in Israel' — the Messianic birthplace prophecy.
'What doth the Lord require of thee, but to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God?' — one of the most quoted verses in the Bible.
'He will turn again, he will have compassion... thou wilt cast all their sins into the depths of the sea' — a beloved image of divine forgiveness.
